title: Wallet / Ledger — System Design
description: Design a money ledger and wallet: hold balances, move funds between accounts, and never lose or invent a cent. The theme is correctness over availability — double-entry accounting, idempotency, concurrency control on balances, and reconciliation.

nodes.label: Client / Service
nodes.description: Initiates transfers, top-ups, and balance reads.

nodes.label: Wallet API
nodes.description: Validates requests, enforces idempotency, orchestrates ledger writes.

nodes.label: Ledger Service
nodes.description: Owns double-entry posting rules and transactional integrity.

nodes.label: Ledger Store
nodes.description: Authoritative, append-only double-entry journal; the source of truth.

nodes.label: Balance View
nodes.description: Materialized per-account balances derived from the journal for fast reads.

nodes.label: Idempotency Store
nodes.description: Maps request idempotency key -> result, so retries don't double-post.

nodes.label: Event Outbox
nodes.description: Transactionally-written events streamed to downstream consumers.

nodes.label: Reconciliation Worker
nodes.description: Recomputes balances from the journal and checks invariants.

nodes.label: Audit / Statement Store
nodes.description: Immutable export of postings for compliance and statements.

nodes.label: External Rail / PSP
nodes.description: Bank/PSP for funding and withdrawals outside the wallet.

functional: Transfer funds between two accounts atomically.
Top up (fund) from and withdraw to an external rail.
Read current balance and transaction history.
Every movement is recorded immutably and is auditable.
nonFunctional: Correctness above all: money is never created, lost, or double-counted.
Idempotent writes: a retried request must not move money twice.
Auditable: a complete, immutable history that always explains a balance.
Read-heavy on balances; writes must be strongly consistent and durable.

api.description: Move funds from one account to another. Idempotent on the provided key.
Read the current balance (from the materialized balance view).

dataModel.name: journal_entries
dataModel.note: Append-only double-entry ledger. The source of truth. Never updated or deleted.

dataModel.name: account_balances
dataModel.note: Derived, materialized balance per account for fast reads.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedup table: a request key -> its committed result.

patterns.name: Double-entry bookkeeping
patterns.what: Every transaction posts equal debits and credits so the books always balance.
patterns.whenToUse: Any money movement that must be provably conserved and auditable.

patterns.name: Immutable append-only ledger
patterns.what: Never mutate or delete a posting; corrections are new compensating entries.
patterns.whenToUse: Auditability and the ability to always reconstruct a balance.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: A client-supplied key the server dedups so a retry doesn't post twice.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving APIs over unreliable networks (at-least-once clients).

patterns.name: Materialized balance view
patterns.what: Maintain a derived per-account balance for O(1) reads instead of summing the journal.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy balance access over a large journal.

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency control
patterns.what: Version the balance row and reject conflicting concurrent updates so two transfers can't lose an update.
patterns.whenToUse: Concurrent balance changes that must not race.

patterns.name: Reconciliation
patterns.what: Periodically recompute balances from the journal and assert invariants.
patterns.whenToUse: Detecting and correcting any drift between derived state and the source of truth.

steps.title: 1. Model Money as a Double-Entry Ledger
description: Don't store a mutable 'balance' field and add/subtract it — that loses history and is impossible to audit. Instead record every movement as balanced double-entry postings: each transaction writes a debit on one account and an equal credit on another.
The journal is append-only and immutable: the source of truth is the full list of postings, and a balance is the sum of an account's entries. Corrections are new compensating entries, never edits.
Use integer minor units (cents) or fixed-point decimals — never floating point — so amounts are exact.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why record paired debit/credit entries instead of just incrementing a balance column?

concepts.definition: Every transaction posts equal and opposite debits and credits across accounts.
concepts.whyItMatters: The sum of all entries is always zero, so money is provably conserved and every balance is explainable.
concepts.example: Transfer $10: debit a_1 $10, credit a_2 $10 in one transaction.

whyNow: The data model is the whole correctness story; leading with an immutable double-entry journal is what a money system requires.

recap.after: An immutable, balanced, auditable journal of all money movement.
recap.newRisk: Summing the journal for every balance read is too slow — need a derived balance and exactness on writes.

traps.trap: Storing a single mutable balance and incrementing it.
traps.why: No history, no audit trail, and concurrent updates silently corrupt it.
traps.instead: Append immutable double-entry postings; derive balances from them.

traps.why: Rounding errors accumulate and the books stop balancing.
traps.instead: Integer minor units or fixed-point decimals.

steps.title: 2. Idempotent Transfers
description: Networks retry. Without protection, a retried transfer posts twice and moves money twice. The client supplies an idempotency key; the API records key -> result in an idempotency store within the same transaction as the posting.
A repeat of the same key returns the original result without posting again. This turns at-least-once clients into effectively-once money movement.
Scope the key to the operation and persist it durably alongside the journal write so a crash can't lose the dedup record while keeping the posting (or vice versa).
steps.decisionPrompt: A transfer succeeds but the response is lost; the client retries. How do you guarantee the money moves exactly once?

concepts.definition: A client-generated key the server uses to dedup retried mutations.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents double-spending from retries — essential for money APIs.
concepts.example: Second POST with key k_9 returns the first transfer's result, no new postings.

whyNow: Idempotency is the second non-negotiable for money; doing it transactionally with the posting is the correctness nuance.

recap.after: Each logical transfer posts at most once.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent transfers on the same account can still race on the derived balance.

failureDrills.scenario: API crashes after appending the journal but before returning.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Client retries with the same key; the idempotency record (written in the same txn) makes the server return the original result.
failureDrills.mitigation: Write idempotency key + postings in one atomic transaction.

steps.title: 3. Materialized Balances for Fast Reads
description: Summing an account's entire journal on every balance read is too slow once history grows. Maintain a derived account_balances row updated in the same transaction as the postings.
Reads hit the materialized balance (O(1)); the journal remains the source of truth, and the balance carries a watermark (last applied entry id) so it can always be recomputed and verified.
Because the balance is derived, it's safe to rebuild from the journal at any time — which is exactly what reconciliation does.
steps.decisionPrompt: If balances are derived, why update them transactionally with the journal instead of asynchronously?
whyNow: Fast balance reads are required, and tying the materialized view to the journal transaction keeps it trustworthy rather than eventually-stale.

concepts.definition: A precomputed balance plus the id of the last journal entry it reflects.
concepts.whyItMatters: O(1) reads while staying verifiable against the source of truth.
concepts.example: balance=42.00, last_entry_id=9981 -> recompute from entries > 9981 to verify.

recap.after: O(1) balance reads; still recomputable from the journal.
recap.newRisk: Two concurrent transfers on the same account can both read-modify-write the balance and lose an update.

steps.title: 4. Concurrency Control on Balances
description: Two transfers debiting the same account at once can both read the old balance and write back conflicting values — a lost update, and possibly an overdraft. The journal stays correct, but the derived balance and any balance-check would be wrong.
Guard the balance update with concurrency control: optimistic (version the row; the second writer's compare-and-set fails and retries) or pessimistic (row lock / serialized per-account writes).
Enforce business invariants here too — e.g. reject a transfer that would overdraw, atomically within the same transaction that checks and decrements.
steps.decisionPrompt: Two $60 withdrawals hit a $100 account simultaneously. How do you prevent both succeeding?

concepts.definition: Read a version, compute, and commit only if the version is unchanged; otherwise retry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents lost updates without holding locks under low contention.
concepts.example: UPDATE balances SET balance=40, version=8 WHERE id=a_1 AND version=7.

whyNow: Concurrency is where a 'correct on paper' ledger actually stays correct under load; naming lost-update + the overdraft invariant is the senior signal.

recap.before: Concurrent transfers could lose updates / overdraw.
recap.after: Per-account writes serialize; invariants enforced atomically.
recap.newRisk: Hot single account becomes a write bottleneck; cross-account/cross-shard transfers need atomicity.

traps.trap: Checking the balance, then decrementing in a separate step.
traps.why: Two requests both pass the check before either decrements -> overdraft.

bottlenecks.issue: A single very active account serializes all its writes.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Acceptable for correctness; for extreme cases, sub-accounts/buckets that periodically net.
Two-phase commit or an orchestrated saga with compensating entries; keep both postings under one logical transaction.

steps.title: 5. Reliable Downstream Events (Outbox)
description: Other systems (notifications, statements, fraud, analytics) need to know about each posting. Don't write the event in a separate step — a crash between the ledger write and the event publish would diverge them.
Use the transactional outbox: write the event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the postings. A relay then streams outbox rows to consumers at-least-once.
Consumers dedup on transaction id (the events are naturally idempotent because the ledger is), so downstream stays consistent with the source of truth.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why not just publish a Kafka event right after committing the ledger write?

concepts.definition: Write domain events into a table within the business transaction; relay them afterward.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees the event exists iff the posting committed — no dual-write divergence.
concepts.example: Posting + outbox row commit together; a relay tails the outbox to the stream.

whyNow: Money systems must never have an event without a posting or vice versa; the outbox is the standard guarantee and reuses the ledger's atomicity.

recap.before: Events published separately could diverge from the ledger.
recap.after: Events are emitted atomically with postings, delivered at-least-once.
recap.newRisk: Derived state (balances, exports) can still drift due to bugs — need a backstop check.

steps.title: 6. Reconciliation, Audit, and Settlement
description: Periodically reconcile: recompute each account's balance from the journal and assert it matches the materialized balance and the system-wide invariant (debits == credits). Any mismatch is an alarm, and the journal is authoritative for repair.
Export immutable statements/audit records for compliance; the append-only journal makes any historical balance reconstructable on demand.

Reconcile against external rails too: funding/withdrawals settle asynchronously through a PSP/bank, so match internal postings to external settlement reports and post compensating entries for any discrepancy.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you detect that a bug caused a materialized balance to drift from the journal?

concepts.definition: Recompute derived state from the source of truth and assert invariants hold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Catches drift and corruption early; the journal is always the authority for correction.
concepts.example: Nightly: sum(entries) per account == account_balances; global debits == credits.

whyNow: Closing on reconciliation + audit + external settlement completes the correctness story: even bugs and external mismatches are detectable and fixable.

recap.before: Derived state trusted blindly; external rails unverified.
recap.after: Balances continuously verified; statements auditable; external settlement reconciled.
recap.newRisk: Reconciliation lag and external settlement delays — bounded by invariant checks and compensating entries.

failureDrills.scenario: An external withdrawal is reported failed after the wallet already debited.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reconciliation flags the mismatch; a compensating credit reverses the debit.
failureDrills.mitigation: Match internal postings to settlement reports; post compensating entries, never edit history.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Double-Entry Wallet Ledger
finalDesign.description: The wallet API enforces idempotency (key -> result) and posts every transfer as balanced double-entry entries into an immutable journal — the source of truth — in a single atomic transaction that also updates the materialized per-account balance (guarded by optimistic concurrency and invariant checks) and writes a transactional outbox event. Balance reads hit the materialized view; a relay streams outbox events at-least-once; a reconciliation worker recomputes balances from the journal, asserts debits==credits, exports immutable statements, and matches external rail settlements, correcting drift only via compensating entries.

functional.how: Materialized balance derived from the journal.
Balanced debit/credit postings in one transaction.

functional.requirement: Top up / withdraw via external rail

nonFunctional.how: Idempotency key dedup, transactional with the posting.
Per-account serialized writes with concurrency control.

say: Establish correctness over availability — this is a money system.
Pin idempotency, strong consistency, and auditability as hard requirements.
Agree balances are read-heavy but writes must be exact.
Model money as an immutable double-entry journal (source of truth).
Add idempotency keys for safe retries.
Materialize balances for fast reads, updated transactionally.
Concurrency control on balances; check-and-decrement atomically.
Reconciliation, audit, and external settlement matching.
Tradeoffs: per-account write serialization, cross-shard transfers (2PC/saga), reconciliation lag.
Mention multi-currency and holds/authorizations as extensions.

expectations: Uses a ledger and avoids mutable balances.
May miss concurrency control and reconciliation.
Reasons about cross-shard atomicity (2PC vs saga + compensation).
Designs reconciliation and external settlement matching.
Discusses hot-account bottlenecks and multi-currency precisely.

followUps: How would you support a transfer across two shards atomically?
How do you handle multi-currency wallets and FX without rounding errors?
How would you implement holds/authorizations (reserve then capture)?
How do you scale a single very hot account's writes?
How do you correct a posting made in error without violating immutability?
